annotations.label: Map projection

anchor.snippet: a map projection is any of a broad set of transformations

annotations.note: Cartographic notion of a map projection from sphere/ellipsoid to plane is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: All sphere-to-plane projections distort

anchor.snippet: All projections of a sphere on a plane necessarily distort the surface

annotations.note: No statement about necessary distortion of sphere-to-plane maps exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Theorema Egregium (sphere not flattenable)

anchor.snippet: Theorema Egregium proved that a sphere's surface cannot be represented on a plane without distortion

annotations.note: Grep for `Theorema` and `Egregium` returns no hits; Gauss's Theorema Egregium is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tissot's indicatrix

anchor.snippet: Nicolas Tissot described how to construct an ellipse that illustrates the amount and orientation of the components of distortion

annotations.note: No occurrences of Tissot in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Developable surface

anchor.snippet: A surface that can be unfolded or unrolled into a plane or sheet without stretching, tearing or shrinking is called a developable surface

annotations.note: No notion of developable surface (zero Gaussian curvature surface) is found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cylinder, cone, plane are developable

anchor.snippet: The cylinder , cone and the plane are all developable surfaces

annotations.note: Without a definition of developable surface, this proposition has no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Sphere and ellipsoid are not developable

anchor.snippet: The sphere and ellipsoid do not have developable surfaces

annotations.note: Mathlib has no developable-surface theory, so this non-developability claim is absent.

annotations.label: Aspect of a projection

anchor.snippet: The aspect describes how the developable surface is placed relative to the globe

annotations.note: Cartographic notion of aspect (normal/transverse/oblique) is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tangent vs secant surface

anchor.snippet: Tangent means the surface touches but does not slice through the globe

annotations.note: Tangency/secancy of an auxiliary projection surface relative to a globe is not a Mathlib concept.

annotations.label: Standard lines / standard parallel

anchor.snippet: Tangent and secant lines ( standard lines ) are represented undistorted

annotations.note: Standard parallel terminology from cartography does not appear in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Central meridian

anchor.snippet: The central meridian is the meridian to which the globe is rotated before projecting

annotations.note: The cartographic notion of central meridian is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Globe is only constant-scale representation

anchor.snippet: A globe is the only way to represent the Earth with constant scale throughout the entire map in all directions

annotations.note: No formal statement about constant-scale representations of the Earth exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conformal map (scale independent of direction)

anchor.snippet: This is equivalent to preservation of angles, the defining characteristic of a conformal map

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `ConformalAt`/`Conformal` as having a conformal-linear differential, capturing angle/scale preservation.

annotations.label: Equidistant projection

anchor.snippet: This is the defining characteristic of an equidistant projection

annotations.note: Equidistant projections as a cartographic class are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: No projection is both equal-area and conformal

anchor.snippet: it is impossible to construct a map projection that is both equal-area and conformal

annotations.note: This incompatibility theorem about sphere-to-plane maps is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Normal cylindrical projection

anchor.snippet: A normal cylindrical projection is any projection in which meridians are mapped to equally spaced vertical lines

annotations.note: No cylindrical map projections defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cylindrical projections stretch east-west by secant of latitude

anchor.snippet: The amount of stretch is the same at any chosen latitude on all cylindrical projections, and is given by the secant of the latitude

annotations.note: Mathlib has no statement about east-west stretch factor in cylindrical projections.

annotations.label: Mercator: north-south stretching equals sec φ

anchor.snippet: The east-west scale matches the north-south scale: conformal cylindrical or Mercator

annotations.note: Grep finds no occurrences of Mercator in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equirectangular / plate carrée

anchor.snippet: North-south distances neither stretched nor compressed (1): equirectangular projection

annotations.note: No equirectangular/plate carrée projection appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equal-area cylindrical

anchor.snippet: North-south compression equals the cosine of the latitude

annotations.note: No equal-area cylindrical projection found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Transverse cylindrical projection

anchor.snippet: A transverse cylindrical projection is a cylindrical projection that in the tangent case uses a great circle along a meridian

annotations.note: Transverse cylindrical projections are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Oblique cylindrical projection

anchor.snippet: An oblique cylindrical projection aligns with a great circle, but not the equator and not a meridian

annotations.note: Oblique cylindrical projections are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pseudocylindrical projection

anchor.snippet: Pseudocylindrical projections represent the central meridian as a straight line segment

annotations.note: No pseudocylindrical projections in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sinusoidal projection

anchor.snippet: Sinusoidal , which was the first pseudocylindrical projection developed

annotations.note: No sinusoidal projection found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: HEALPix projection

anchor.snippet: The HEALPix projection combines an equal-area cylindrical projection in equatorial regions with the Collignon projection in polar areas

annotations.note: HEALPix/Collignon are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conic projection

anchor.snippet: The term conic projection is used to refer to any projection in which meridians are mapped to equally spaced lines radiating out from the apex

annotations.note: Conic projections are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equidistant conic

anchor.snippet: Equidistant conic , which keeps parallels evenly spaced along the meridians

annotations.note: Equidistant conic projection is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Albers conic

anchor.snippet: Albers conic , which adjusts the north-south distance between non-standard parallels to compensate for the east-west stretching

annotations.note: No Albers projection in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lambert conformal conic

anchor.snippet: Lambert conformal conic , which adjusts the north-south distance between non-standard parallels to equal the east-west stretching

annotations.note: Lambert conformal conic projection is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bonne projection

anchor.snippet: Bonne , an equal-area projection on which most meridians and parallels appear as curved lines

annotations.note: No Bonne projection in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Werner cordiform

anchor.snippet: Werner cordiform , upon which distances are correct from one pole

annotations.note: No Werner cordiform projection in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Azimuthal projection

anchor.snippet: Azimuthal projections have the property that directions from a central point are preserved

annotations.note: Azimuthal projections are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gnomonic projection

anchor.snippet: The gnomonic projection displays great circles as straight lines

annotations.note: No gnomonic projection found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Orthographic projection

anchor.snippet: The orthographic projection maps each point on the Earth to the closest point on the plane

annotations.note: No orthographic projection of the sphere is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Near-sided perspective projection

anchor.snippet: Near-sided perspective projection, which simulates the view from space at a finite distance

annotations.note: Near-sided perspective projection is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: General Perspective projection

anchor.snippet: The General Perspective projection can be constructed by using a point of perspective outside the Earth

annotations.note: General Perspective projection is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stereographic projection

anchor.snippet: The stereographic projection , which is conformal, can be constructed by using the tangent point's antipode as the point of perspective

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `stereographic` as the stereographic projection from the unit sphere in an inner product space, centered at a unit vector.

annotations.label: Azimuthal equidistant

anchor.snippet: Distance from the tangent point on the map is proportional to surface distance on the Earth

annotations.note: Azimuthal equidistant projection is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lambert azimuthal equal-area

anchor.snippet: Distance from the tangent point on the map is proportional to straight-line distance through the Earth

annotations.note: Lambert azimuthal equal-area projection is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Logarithmic azimuthal

anchor.snippet: Logarithmic azimuthal is constructed so that each point's distance from the center of the map is the logarithm of its distance from the tangent point

annotations.note: Logarithmic azimuthal projection is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Polyhedral map projection

anchor.snippet: Polyhedral map projections use a polyhedron to subdivide the globe into faces

annotations.note: Polyhedral map projections are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conformal map projection

anchor.snippet: Conformal , or orthomorphic, map projections preserve angles locally

annotations.note: Mathlib's `ConformalAt`/`Conformal` capture the angle-preserving property abstractly, but not specifically applied to sphere-to-plane map projections.

annotations.label: Equal-area map

anchor.snippet: Equal-area maps preserve area measure, generally distorting shapes in order to do so

annotations.note: The cartographic notion of equal-area projection is not isolated in Mathlib (measure-preserving maps exist generally but not in this geographic context).

annotations.label: Equidistant projection (distance preservation)

anchor.snippet: An equidistant projection preserves distances from one or two special points to all other points

annotations.note: Equidistant projections (distances from special points) are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two-point equidistant

anchor.snippet: Two-point equidistant : Two "control points" are arbitrarily chosen by the map maker

annotations.note: Two-point equidistant projection is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gnomonic (straight-line great circles)

anchor.snippet: Great circles are displayed as straight lines: Gnomonic projection

annotations.note: Gnomonic projection is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Retroazimuthal projection

anchor.snippet: Direction to a fixed location B (the bearing at the starting location A of the shortest route) corresponds to the direction on the map from A to B

annotations.note: Retroazimuthal projections are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Compromise projection

anchor.snippet: Compromise projections give up the idea of perfectly preserving metric properties

annotations.note: Compromise projections (Winkel tripel, Robinson, etc.) are not in Mathlib.
